content.problemStatement: Recently Chef bought a bunch of robot-waiters. And now he needs to know how much to pay for the electricity that robots use for their work. All waiters serve food from the kitchen (which is in the point **(0, 0)**) and carry it to some table (which is in some point **(x, y)**) in a shortest way. But this is a beta version of robots and they can only do the next moves: turn right and make a step forward or turn left and make a step forward. Initially they look in direction of **X-axis**. Your task is to calculate for each query the number of moves they’ll do to reach corresponding table.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two space-separated integers x and y.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of moves the robot will make to reach the point (x, y).

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the robot needs to move 3 steps to the right and 3 steps up, so the total number of moves is 6. For the second test case, the robot needs to move 3 steps to the right and 4 steps up, so the total number of moves is 7.
The robot needs to move 3 steps to the left and 4 steps up, so the total number of moves is 7.
The robot is already at the target point, so the total number of moves is 0.

solution.approach: The key insight is that the robot can only move in two directions: right and up. Since the robot starts at the origin (0, 0) and can only move right or up, the number of moves it takes to reach a point (x, y) is simply the sum of the absolute values of x and y. This is because the robot must move |x| times to the right and |y| times up to reach the point (x, y).
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) per test case, O(T) total. No precomputation is needed.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space used does not grow with the size of the input.

code.Python3:
import sys
T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    x, y = map(int, input().split())
    moves = abs(x) + abs(y)
    print(moves)
